the gap how it works what they found does it hold up? takeaways
2018 · arXiv · Post-training & RL

World Models

A VAE compresses each frame, an MDN-RNN predicts what comes next, and a tiny linear controller learns to act — trained almost entirely inside its own hallucinated dream.

David Ha, Jürgen Schmidhuber — Google Brain, NNAISENSE, IDSIA (USI & SUPSI)
venue arXiv citations 1,891 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07 (Semantic Scholar)
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Standard deep RL pipelines — DQN, A3C, and their relatives — learn a policy end to end from raw pixels, and the credit-assignment problem that comes with that setup means the policy network is usually kept small: it's hard to backpropagate a sparse, delayed reward signal through millions of weights, so in practice people use small networks that iterate fast rather than the large, expressive ones deep learning is otherwise good at training. That's backwards from how the rest of deep learning works, where bigger networks trained with plain backprop against a well-behaved differentiable loss keep winning.

Ha & Schmidhuber's reframing: split the agent into a large world model, trained first and entirely unsupervised on collected rollouts, and a small controller that only has to map the world model's compact representation to actions. The controller is small enough — a few hundred to a couple thousand parameters — that it doesn't need gradient-based RL at all; it can be evolved with black-box search. And once the world model can predict what the environment will do next, the controller doesn't need the real environment to train against — it can be optimized entirely inside a rollout hallucinated by the model itself, touching the actual simulator only to gather the original training data and, at the end, to check the learned policy actually transfers.

// how it works

The method

The agent is three components, trained in sequence rather than jointly. V (Vision) is a convolutional Variational Autoencoder that compresses each raw observation frame into a small latent vector z — 32 dimensions for the CarRacing task, 64 for VizDoom — by minimizing reconstruction error between a frame and its decoded version. M (Memory) is an LSTM with a Mixture Density Network output head (an "MDN-RNN," borrowed directly from Graves' handwriting generation and Ha's own earlier SketchRNN): instead of predicting the next latent vector deterministically, it outputs the parameters of a Gaussian mixture over zt+1, conditioned on the action taken and the RNN's own hidden state. For the VizDoom task, M is also trained to jointly predict a binary "done" signal — whether the agent has died in the next frame — which is what turns M into a complete, self-contained environment simulator rather than just a next-frame predictor.

C (Controller) is deliberately as small as possible: a single linear layer with no hidden units, mapping the concatenation of V's current code and M's hidden state directly to an action. For CarRacing that's just 867 parameters; for VizDoom, 1,088 — against 4.35M (V) and 422K (M) parameters on the CarRacing side, and 4.45M (V) and 1.68M (M) on the VizDoom side. Because nearly all of the model's capacity lives in V and M, and because C is small enough that it sits in a solution space of only a few thousand dimensions, the authors optimize it with the Covariance-Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy (CMA-ES) — a population-based black-box search — rather than any gradient-based RL algorithm, evolved on a single machine across parallel rollouts.

M models  P(zt+1 | at, zt, ht)  ·  C computes  at = Wc [zt ht] + bc // a temperature τ reshapes the mixture's sampling variance at rollout time — turning it up makes M's imagined future noisier and harder to exploit

The pivotal move: once M is trained, it is an environment — wrap it in a standard Gym interface (feed it an action, get back a sampled zt+1 and, for VizDoom, a death flag) and C can be evolved entirely against these hallucinated rollouts, with V's decoder only needed to render the dream back to pixels for a human to watch. No real environment steps are required once the initial random-policy dataset (10,000 rollouts) has been collected.

// what they found

The results

CarRacing-v0 is solved when an agent's average score over 100 random tracks exceeds 900. The full V+M+C agent reached 906 ± 21 — by the authors' account, the first reported solution to clear that bar — ahead of the best entry then on the OpenAI Gym leaderboard (838 ± 11) and well past the A3C and DQN baselines reported by other groups on the same task. Ablating the controller's inputs shows why M matters: with access to only V's code zt (no memory), the agent drives but wobbles on sharp corners, scoring 632 ± 251; adding a hidden layer to that handicapped controller helps (788 ± 141) but still falls short. Only once the controller can also see M's hidden state ht — which carries information about the distribution of likely futures — does driving become stable enough to solve the task, without the agent ever explicitly planning or rolling out hypothetical trajectories at decision time.

MethodAvg. score (100 trials)
DQN343 ± 18
A3C (continuous)591 ± 45
A3C (discrete)652 ± 10
Gym leaderboard (best prior)838 ± 11
V model only (zt)632 ± 251
V model + hidden layer788 ± 141
Full world model (V+M+C)906 ± 21

VizDoom: Take Cover is solved when average survival time over 100 rollouts exceeds 750 steps (~20s), out of a 2,100-step (~60s) episode cap. Here the entire controller is trained inside "DoomRNN" — M wrapped as a latent-space-only Gym environment, with no rendering and no VizDoom engine running — reaching roughly 900 steps of survival inside the dream itself, using a raised temperature (τ = 1.15) that makes the hallucinated fireballs move less predictably than the real game, deliberately making the dream harder than reality. Deployed back into the real, never-trained-on VizDoom engine, the same policy survives 1,092 ± 556 steps over 100 consecutive trials — comfortably past the 750-step bar and, notably, higher than its own score inside the harder dream, evidence that the learned behavior generalizes rather than merely overfitting the hallucination.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The paper is refreshingly honest about the sharpest weakness of its own approach, in a section literally titled "Cheating the World Model": if the temperature used to sample M's predicted distribution is left too low (e.g. τ = 0.1), CMA-ES discovers an adversarial controller that exploits M's imperfections directly — moving in a way that makes the hallucinated monsters never fire a single fireball during some rollouts, an "ability" that has nothing to do with the real game and would fail immediately if deployed there. Raising τ is the fix the authors land on, and it works, but it's a hyperparameter band-aid on a structural problem: any controller trained purely against a learned model can, in principle, find and exploit that model's blind spots rather than solving the actual task, and the paper only demonstrates one way of dialing that risk down, not a way of eliminating it.

The two testbeds are also both about as forgiving as this technique gets. VizDoom "Take Cover" has one binary "am I alive" signal and a handful of actions in a static-camera hallway — simple enough that M can be trained to predict episode termination jointly with the next latent state, which is exactly the trick that lets the environment be fully replaced by the model. CarRacing-v0 has a single continuous reward stream and no other agents to model. Neither task tests long-horizon credit assignment, rich reward shaping, or an environment where the model's one-step-ahead training objective has to compound accurately over many steps of imagined rollout — the standard failure mode of learned dynamics models used for planning. The baselines (DQN, A3C) are also taken from other groups' reported numbers rather than reproduced in-house under identical frame preprocessing, so the "906 vs. 838" leaderboard comparison is a fair top-line claim but not a strictly controlled one.

baselines

DQN/A3C numbers are cited from prior work on the same OpenAI Gym leaderboard rather than re-run by the authors under matched preprocessing; the Gym-leaderboard comparison (838 ± 11) is the more apples-to-apples one.

contamination

Not applicable in the data-leakage sense, but the paper's own "cheating the world model" section is a documented case of the controller overfitting to defects in the learned dynamics model rather than the real task.

what's missed

Only two small, single-agent, short-horizon environments; no test of how imagined-rollout error compounds over long horizons, and no continuous-control locomotion task.

would raise confidence

A reproduced, matched-preprocessing RL baseline; an environment with denser/shaped reward and a larger action space; an explicit measurement of imagination-vs-reality divergence as rollout length grows.

// bottom line

Read this as a proof-of-concept and a landmark of alternative science communication — the original is an interactive, distill.pub-style HTML essay with live τ and z sliders, only later fixed into this arXiv preprint (and Zenodo-archived, since it was never run through peer review as a conventional paper) — rather than a scalable algorithm in its own right. Its tiny linear controller and CMA-ES optimizer were both superseded almost immediately by its own direct descendants: PlaNet's latent-space planning and, further down the line, Dreamer/DreamerV3's much larger actor-critic networks trained by backpropagating through imagined rollouts. That every one of this paper's central claims (compress with a VAE, predict the future with a recurrent world model, train the policy in imagination) survived into a decade of increasingly capable successors is the strongest evidence the core idea was right, even though this exact 2018 instantiation of it wasn't the one that ultimately scaled.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Split a large, unsupervised world model (VAE + MDN-RNN) from a tiny, evolvable controller: the controller is small enough to optimize with black-box search (CMA-ES) instead of gradient-based RL, sidestepping the credit-assignment bottleneck that keeps most RL policy networks small.
  2. Once M can predict both the next latent state and whether the episode has ended, it doubles as a complete, self-contained Gym environment — the controller can be trained almost entirely "in a dream," touching the real simulator only to collect the initial random rollouts and to verify transfer at the end.
  3. The sampling temperature τ is a direct knob on realism vs. exploitability: raise it and the dream gets noisier and harder, which is exactly what stops the controller from learning to cheat the learned model's blind spots — a concern every later Dreamer-line successor still has to manage in some form.
